heroSubtitle: The Untold Story of Ethiopia's Warriors who Fought in the Korean War
heroDescription: In 1951, Emperor Haile Selassie sent Ethiopia's finest soldiers to a war most of the world has forgotten. No African nation had ever done this before. Their story has never been fully told — until now.
storyParagraph1: In 1951, Emperor Haile Selassie dispatched thousands of soldiers — drawn from his elite Imperial Bodyguard, the Kebur Zabagna — to fight alongside American-led UN forces in Korea. They were the Kagnew Battalions, and they became one of the most decorated units of the entire war.
storyParagraph2: Six thousand Ethiopians crossed the world to fight for the freedom of people they had never met. They suffered 121 killed and 536 wounded in battle. Not a single soldier was ever captured.
storyParagraph3: This was the only time in history that an African nation voluntarily sent its own soldiers to fight outside the continent. Their valor is extraordinary. Their story is almost entirely unknown.

timelineItems.label: UN Intervention
timelineItems.desc: U.S.-led UN forces enter the war

timelineItems.label: Ethiopia Deploys
timelineItems.desc: First Kagnew Battalion arrives in Korea

timelineItems.label: Kagnew in Combat
timelineItems.desc: Ethiopian troops fight in 250+ battles alongside UN forces

timelineItems.label: Major Engagements
timelineItems.desc: Kagnew Battalion fights in key battles including Old Baldy

timelineItems.label: Armistice Signed
timelineItems.desc: Korean Armistice Agreement signed, war ends

timelineItems.label: Kagnew Legacy Continues
timelineItems.desc: Additional Ethiopian rotations remain in Korea post-war

blockquoteText: We were the best fighters. The three Ethiopian battalions fought 253 battles, and no Ethiopian soldier was taken prisoner in the Korean War. That was our Ethiopian motto: 'Never be captured on the war field.'
blockquoteCitation: — Captain Mamo Habtewold, 81 · Lieutenant, 3rd Kagnew Battalion
whyNowHeading: Before Their Voices Are Lost Forever
whyNowParagraph1: The remaining Kagnew Battalion veterans are aging quickly. Many can no longer speak. Each day we wait, we risk losing their firsthand accounts — forever.
whyNowParagraph2: This documentary will, for the first time, tell the Korean War through three perspectives: the Ethiopian Kagnew soldiers, the Korean fighters, and the American servicemen — revealing a shared history that has never been fully seen or heard.
whyNowParagraph3: This is our last chance to preserve their legacy. Stories of courage and sacrifice that must not be forgotten.

filmmakers.name: Aïda Bekele
filmmakers.title: Executive Producer
filmmakers.bio: An Ethiopian educator, organizer, and humanitarian — and daughter of Major Bekele Abebe, a Kagnew Battalion soldier. Aida teaches at the International Community School in Ethiopia and is fluent in English, Amharic, French, and Spanish. Her lived connection to this history ensures the film is grounded, authentic, and driven by truth and human dignity.

filmmakers.name: Bemnet Yemesgen
filmmakers.title: Director & Producer
filmmakers.bio: A filmmaker, photographer, and creative director who has worked with Adidas, USA Today, and the Nike Foundation. When Bemnet discovered that two of his uncles had served in the Korean War, he realized he knew almost nothing about Ethiopia's role — and that almost no one else did either. That discovery became the catalyst for this film.
